ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Sloppy Joe; mapped to the broader 'sandwich food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Sloppy Joe entry; A0CKP (meat-based composite dish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Sloppy Joe as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Sloppy Joe is approximately 200 g, comprising a soft burger bun filled with seasoned ground beef bolognese sauce, melted fior di latte, red cheddar, and grana padano.

nutrient_source.title: Sloppy Joe — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as sourced from HPB Foodo. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef sloppy joe (USDA #21299) as a supplementary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sloppy Joe as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a white burger bun (GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database) blended with a high-protein, moderate-fat beef filling that attenuates glycaemic response. The composite GI is estimated using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method: bun (~50% of carbohydrate, GI 70) and filling (~50%, GI ~40 for meat-sauce component with vegetables), yielding a blended estimate of ~55. Glycaemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 18.43 × 2) / 100 = 20.3, rounded to 20.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 343 mg/100 g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 686 mg sodium, representing ~30% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation — specifically less Worcestershire sauce and tomato sauce — and avoid adding pickles or high-sodium condiments; the potassium content (253 mg/100 g) provides partial offsetting benefit but does not eliminate the sodium concern at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (18.4 g/100 g) with estimated GI 55; a 200 g serving yields glycaemic load ~20, which is at the upper boundary of the moderate GL range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a wholegrain bun to lower the glycaemic index and improve fibre intake; the high protein content (11.85 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g) slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glucose rise, but portion control to one serving is advised to keep GL within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g estimated) from ground beef and cheese components; sodium 343 mg/100 g; cholesterol 26 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to one serving, request leaner ground beef or a turkey substitute to reduce saturated fat intake, and avoid extra cheese toppings; the moderate sodium content warrants caution in those with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ground beef contributes arachidonic acid, a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids; trace trans fat from processed components is additionally pro-inflammatory per EULAR guidelines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting ground turkey or chicken to reduce arachidonic acid load, request a wholegrain bun, and be aware that bell peppers and tomato are nightshade vegetables to which some RA patients report individual sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sloppy Joe is a classic American open-faced or bun-based sandwich consisting of seasoned ground beef cooked in a tangy tomato-based sauce, served on a soft burger bun. This Singapore-adapted version features fior di latte (fresh mozzarella), red cheddar, grana padano, and bell peppers alongside the beef bolognese filling, giving it a richer, more cheese-forward flavour profile than the traditional American preparation. The dish is warm, saucy, and slightly sweet-savoury, with a soft bun texture contrasting the tender, saucy meat filling. Nutritionally, it provides 180 kcal per 100 g with a strong protein contribution of 11.85 g, moderate carbohydrate of 18.43 g, and relatively low fat of 6.44 g. Dietary fibre at 2.2 g/100 g and potassium at 253 mg/100 g are notable positive attributes. Sodium at 343 mg/100 g is a moderate concern for hypertension management. The estimated glycaemic index of 55 (low) means blood glucose impact is moderate, and the high protein content helps slow gastric emptying. The dish is suitable as a lunch or dinner option and is commonly found in Western casual dining and school canteen settings in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is Sloppy Joe suitable for diabetics?
Is Sloppy Joe high in protein?
Can I eat Sloppy Joe if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 for all reported fields. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous entry. GI is an analogous estimate using the University of Sydney GI Database weighted carbohydrate method, which is the standard approved approach for composite dishes without a direct GI measurement.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from 'Beverages / Coffee' (input error) to 'Mixed / Sandwiches and filled buns', which accurately reflects the dish type. 2. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef sloppy joe (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB did not report this field; this is documented as an adapted value. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are not available from a directly applicable approved source without further laboratory analysis. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sloppy Joe exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. This dish is Western cuisine originating in the United States; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. English-only alias coverage is appropriate. 6. Halal status is not confirmed as the beef sourcing and preparation method are not certified in the input record.
